retrieved_docs.title: Bleomycin -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Since early clinical trials in the 1960s, bleomycin pulmonary toxicity (BPT) has been a recognized adverse effect of this drug. Recent studies have described BPT rates of approximately 10% in patients taking bleomycin, with 14% of these BPT cases proving fatal. For this reason, careful monitoring for toxicities accompanied by bleomycin levels is essential. As previously described, BPT can include a serious condition known as pulmonary fibrosis. Risk factors for BPT include cumulative dose, raised creatinine, advanced age, supplemental oxygen, and reduced glomerular filtration rate. While many cases of BPT are irreversible or fatal, evidence suggests that in some surviving patients, pulmonary parameters can improve to baseline in approximately two years. Although there are no well-established therapies for reversing BPT, studies involving alternative formulations of the drug have shown promise. Numerous studies have also demonstrated that bleomycin can sometimes be substituted for less toxic chemotherapy and immunotherapy agents as a part of a multi-drug regimen, producing similar outcomes. This approach is especially useful for patients with multiple BPT risk factors and patients whose low-grade disease does not merit the risk of BPT. [11] In addition to substituting less toxic drugs for bleomycin in chemotherapy regimens, efforts to reduce the risk of pulmonary damage have also included the investigation of lipophilic bleomycin analogs, such as liblomycin. These investigations have not yielded promising results in animal models. [13] Thus, it remains to be seen as to whether the role of bleomycin will continue to diminish in the setting of chemotherapeutic treatment regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: Bleomycin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of bleomycin toxicity. Review the risk factors for developing bleomycin pulmonary toxicity. Summarize appropriate monitoring of bleomycin therapy. Outline some interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication when administering bleomycin. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Bleomycin -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: The most common serious adverse effect of bleomycin is pulmonary toxicity, often referred to as bleomycin pulmonary toxicity or BPT. This adverse effect sometimes leads to pulmonary fibrosis, a chronic and irreversible disease with a poor prognosis. In observing the development of pulmonary fibrosis, inflammatory cell infiltration into pulmonary endothelial cells is seen after one week of exposure to bleomycin, and fibrotic changes with elevated collagen content are seen after three weeks of exposure to bleomycin. Other changes include increased expression of fibrogenic mediators such as transforming growth factor (TGF)-beta, connective tissue growth factor, and platelet-derived growth factor (PGDF)-C in endothelial cells exposed to bleomycin. Additionally, thapsigargin-induced prostaglandin I2 and nitric oxide, which are both vasodilatory agents, are seen to decrease in endothelial pneumocytes exposed to bleomycin. The administration of bleomycin is thus seen to induce functional changes in endothelial cells of the lung leading to respiratory damage, although the exact mechanism of these changes is not entirely understood. Other adverse reactions include fever, chills, faintness, chest pain, and shortness of breath. Less serious reactions include skin pigmentation changes, itching, hypogeusia, rash, nausea, vomiting, and weight loss. Some of these symptoms appear to correlate with a hypersensitivity-type reaction. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Bleomycin -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Although absolute contraindications for bleomycin have not been established, it is crucial to assess lung disease history and renal function before administration. Because of the aforementioned numerous adverse effects of bleomycin on pulmonary tissue, patients with a history of smoking are at elevated risk for pulmonary complications due to bleomycin. In addition, elderly patients and patients with stage IV disease have demonstrated to be more likely to experience lung toxicity with bleomycin exposure. This is also true of patients who receive bolus drug delivery as opposed to continuous infusion. Patients who require supplemental oxygen administration have also been shown to be at increased risk for serious adverse effects of bleomycin exposure. Although studies regarding teratogenic effects of bleomycin exposure in humans are limited, some sources consider antineoplastic therapy during gestation to be generally harmful to fetal development, especially during the first trimester. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Bleomycin -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: As well as checking vital signs and monitoring laboratory values commonly measured during chemotherapeutic treatment, such as liver enzymes, blood cell counts, plasma proteins, and electrolytes, recommendations are that physicians acquire periodic chest imaging of patients receiving bleomycin. This is due to the high risk of pulmonary toxicity associated with the drug and the ability to detect signs of resultant fibrosis on a number of imaging modalities. Such imaging modalities include magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), and plain film X-rays. However, it bears mentioning that according to some sources, imaging alone is considered a nonspecific test for detecting bleomycin pulmonary toxicity, and additional diagnostic tools may be required. In addition to imaging, baseline and post-treatment pulmonary function tests are often part of the patient treatment and monitoring plan. [12] Such pulmonary function tests typically involve measuring a number of respiratory characteristics including lung volume, the quantity of gas exchange, and airflow rates.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Contraindicated Chemotherapeutic Agents -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary toxicity is the dose-limiting toxicity of bleomycin therapy with a reported incidence of approximately 4%, and a mortality rate as high as 20 to 25%. Risk factors for this complication include the cumulative dose of medication, patient age, bleomycin use in multi-drug regimens, particularly with cyclophosphamide, concurrent use of radiation therapy, and supplemental oxygen therapy. [2] Experimental studies with rats produced data that showed synergism in pulmonary toxicity when rats had exposure to 100% oxygen and bleomycin compared to control or bleomycin alone. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Contraindicated Chemotherapeutic Agents -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: However, despite this theoretical risk, there have been no documented cases of pulmonary bleomycin toxicity from hyperbaric oxygen therapy. Alternatively, many patients who have had previous treatment with bleomycin have successfully received treatment with hyperbaric oxygen without sequelae. There is no firmly established timeline for when oxygen therapy appears to be safe after bleomycin administration, but, there is a consensus that as long as the patient has no signs of pulmonary compromise from fibrosis and that is has been at least three to four months since treatment with bleomycin, exposure to hyperbaric oxygen should not be considered a contraindication.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Contraindicated Chemotherapeutic Agents -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Bleomycin is inactivated within cells by the enzyme aminohydrolase. This enzyme has extensive distribution throughout the body; however, it is only present in low concentrations within the skin and the lungs, which explains the predominant toxicities of bleomycin at those sites. Drug-induced pulmonary diseases cover the spectrum of different pathophysiologic conditions of the respiratory tract. Bleomycin is the best-studied cytotoxic pulmonary toxin.

retrieved_docs.title: Bleomycin -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Due to its low therapeutic index and high toxicity, the administration of bleomycin should only be performed in dedicated treatment centers under expert supervision with an interprofessional team. Improper management of patients at high risk for adverse effects, including those with pulmonary and renal dysfunction, can lead to avoidable instances of drug-related harm and death. For these reasons, regular interprofessional communication involving online charting tools, verbal confirmation of prescribed dosages and infusion rates, and regular patient monitoring must take place. Clinicians, pharmacists, and nursing staff should be knowledgeable regarding individual patient histories and risk factors; whenever possible, these interprofessional team members should have specialty oncology training. Notably, patients with previous bleomycin treatment are predisposed to developing rapid pulmonary deterioration due to the sensitization of pulmonary tissue to oxygen. As with other chemotherapy agents, close monitoring of patients' symptoms during treatment with bleomycin is necessary. Monitoring measures include pulmonary function tests, regular lab value assessment, and periodic chest radiographs. [14] [Level 1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Complications -- Issues of Concern -- Use of Chemotherapeutic Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Bleomycin: The current recommendation is to wait at least 6 months after bleomycin administration before receiving hyperbaric oxygen therapy due to the increased risk of complications associated with pulmonary oxygen therapy. In addition, patients who have received bleomycin at any time in the past should undergo pulmonary investigations before initiating hyperbaric oxygen therapy, given the risk of fibrosis associated with the drug. Doxorubicin: The current recommendation is to wait at least 24 hours after doxorubicin administration before initiating hyperbaric oxygen therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Bleomycin -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Bleomycin -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Bleomycin belongs to a subfamily of glycopeptide antibiotics and is utilized primarily as an antineoplastic agent. Glycopeptide antibiotics, a drug class that includes vancomycin, are derived from bacterial species and contain glycosylated peptide cores which are thought to contribute to their mechanism of action in disrupting cellular functions. Bleomycin is part of combination cytotoxic chemotherapy regimens, including ABVD ([adriamycin], doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) and BEACOPP (bleomycin, etoposide, adriamycin, cyclophosphamide, oncovin, procarbazine, prednisone). [1] It first received approval from the FDA in 1975 for the treatment of squamous cell carcinomas, malignant lymphomas, and testicular cancers. [2] Bleomycin has since received a wide array of FDA-approved therapeutic indications, including germinal cell tumors, gestational trophoblastic disease, Hodgkin lymphoma, and non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Non-FDA-approved indications include AIDS-associated Kaposi sarcoma, osteosarcoma, malignant melanoma, and advanced stages of mycosis fungoides. [3] Another non-FDA-approved indication for bleomycin is pleural effusion in patients requiring chemical pleurodesis due to metastatic disease. In pleurodesis, bleomycin can be injected into the pleural space, causing the lung to adhere to the chest wall, thus preventing the collection of fluid or air. Pleurodesis is typically reserved for use in patients with recurrent malignant effusions after more conservative management, such as repeat thoracocentesis, has proven unsuccessful in preventing effusion recurrence. [4] Due to factors discussed below, including the high risk of adverse effects with this drug, newer chemotherapy regimens including less dangerous alternatives to bleomycin and chemotherapy in combination with biologics or targeted immunotherapies are increasingly preferred in many cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Mitomycin -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary toxicity is a rare but well-documented adverse effect of mitomycin C. In one trial at the Mayo Clinic, despite pre-medication with the use of corticosteroids, pulmonary toxicity occurred, with changes in diffusing lung capacity of 9 and PaO2 of 49 mm Hg. A significant number of patients who had bronchoscopies had changes noted on pulmonary histology consistent with lung injury. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Treatment of Radiation Proctitis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy is a relatively low-risk therapy. Otic barotrauma is common but usually not severe. Infrequent complications include oxygen toxicity seizures, transient myopia, and rare cataract formation. Patients with severe structural lung disease or poor left ventricular function are susceptible to more serious complications such as pulmonary barotrauma and acute pulmonary edema. Contraindications to hyperbaric therapy include recent use of doxorubicin or bleomycin, claustrophobia, epilepsy, severe emphysema particularly with blebs, and certain implanted devices.

retrieved_docs.title: Oxygen Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The phenomenon of pulmonary toxicity is commonly referred to as the Smith effect.  This can occur after prolonged exposure to oxygen greater than 0.5 ATA.  The incidence of displaying pulmonary symptoms with oxygen toxicity is 5%.  Preterm newborns are at distinct risk for bronchopulmonary dysplasia and retrolental fibroplasia with prolonged exposure to high concentrations of oxygen. Some chemicals, such as the chemotherapeutic agent bleomycin, also increase the risk of oxygen toxicity [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Soft Tissue Radionecrosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of hyperbaric oxygen therapy includes hypoglycemia in diabetic patients, especially if taking insulin and/or hypoglycemic agents, barotrauma to the ears, pneumothorax, and oxygen toxicity seizures and rarely pulmonary oxygen toxicity. Cancer patients who have a history of having had bleomycin chemotherapy are at increased risk of pulmonary fibrosis even though the chemotherapy was administered remotely from getting the hyperbaric oxygen therapy. This has been a relative contraindication, but some physicians will treat the patient if the benefits outweigh the risk. A patient actively receiving cis-platin is at high risk of bladder toxicity if also getting hyperbaric oxygen therapy and this a contraindication. Also, a relative contraindication is if the patient has had a history of spontaneous pneumothorax with underlying lung disease with air trapping. Chest x-ray, CT scan, and Xenon washout nuclear medicine study can be done to look for blebs, pulmonary disease, pneumothorax, and air trapping. As with any procedure, the risk of the treatment must be weighed against the benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Antifibrinolytics, bleomycin, cardiac glycosides, chromonar, coagulation factors, and insulin Lispro are all recognized as allowed qualifiers when describing the effects of radiation. Each of these agents may be referenced in the context of radiation‑related outcomes, indicating that they are permissible modifiers or considerations in discussions of radiation effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other cytotoxic antibiotics” comprises agents that are used for their cytotoxic properties in cancer treatment, and both bleomycin and mithramycin are classified as members of this group. By being placed together in the “Other cytotoxic antibiotics in ATC” class, bleomycin and mithramycin are recognized as related cytotoxic antibiotics despite having distinct chemical structures and mechanisms of action, underscoring their shared role in oncologic pharmacotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1852
retrieved_docs.content: providing a targeted therapy for Hodgkin lymphoma and systemic anaplastic large‑cell lymphoma. Doxorubicin hydrochloride is an anthracycline chemotherapy agent that intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, while its liposomal formulation (doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome) offers altered pharmacokinetics and reduced cardiotoxicity for various solid tumors and hematologic malignancies. Pembrolizumab is a programmed death‑1 (PD‑1) immune checkpoint inhibitor that enhances antitumor immunity in a wide range of cancers, including melanoma, non‑small cell lung cancer, and urothelial carcinoma. Sargramostim is a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor (GM‑CSF) used to accelerate recovery of white blood cells after chemotherapy or bone‑marrow transplantation. Vinblastine sulfate and vinorelbin tartarát are vinca alkaloid chemotherapeutics that disrupt microtubule formation, with vinblastine commonly used for Hodgkin lymphoma and testicular cancer, and vinorelbine

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 461
retrieved_docs.content: Bleomycin may be used to treat non‑Hodgkin’s lymphoma, which is classified as ورم الغدد اللمفاوية (غير هودجكين). This connection underscores the potential therapeutic role of bleomycin in managing this specific type of lymphoid malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
retrieved_docs.content: corticosteroid potency categories inform dermatologic therapy and how podophyllotoxin‑derived antineoplastic drugs such as etoposide and teniposid are classified, employed in specific chemotherapy protocols, and linked to their underlying mutagenic chemistry.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorambucil, cisplatina, lomustin, mechlorethamin, vincristine sulfate, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome, pembrolizumab, streptozocin, and vinblastine sulfate are therapeutic agents that may be employed in the treatment of مرض هودجكين (Hodgkin disease). These drugs represent a diverse array of pharmacologic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, lomustin, mechlorethamin, streptozocin), a platinum compound (cisplatina), vinca alkaloids (vincristine sulfate, vinblastine sulfate), a bleomycin family agent (bleomycin sulfate), an anthracycline formulation (doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome), a monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate (brentuximab vedotin), and an immune checkpoint inhibitor (pembrolizumab). Their combined or sequential use reflects the multimodal approach to managing Hodgkin disease, aiming to eradicate malignant Reed‑Sternberg cells, achieve durable remission, and minimize disease‑related morbidity. Selection of a specific regimen is guided by disease stage, prior therapies, and individual patient tolerance, underscoring the importance of personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2712
retrieved_docs.content: Alkylating agents constitute a class of chemotherapeutic drugs that exert their antineoplastic effect by adding alkyl groups to DNA, causing cross‑linking and ultimately cell death. Mitomycin is an example of an alkylating agent, illustrating the typical properties of this drug class. Because agents within this class share similar mechanisms of action and overlapping toxicities, the concurrent use of cyclophosphamide—a drug also belonging to the alkylating agent category—with other alkylating agents is generally contraindicated. This contraindication reflects the need to avoid additive adverse effects, such as heightened bone‑marrow suppression and organ toxicity, when multiple alkylating agents are administered together. Clinicians therefore carefully consider drug selection and avoid combining cyclophosphamide with other alkylating agents like mitomycin to ensure safer patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1852
retrieved_docs.content: sulfate and vinorelbin tartarát are vinca alkaloid chemotherapeutics that disrupt microtubule formation, with vinblastine commonly used for Hodgkin lymphoma and testicular cancer, and vinorelbine primarily indicated for non‑small cell lung cancer and breast cancer. Together, these substances illustrate the breadth of pharmacologic interventions—from hormonal agents and central nervous system depressants to cytotoxic chemotherapies, targeted biologics, and immunomodulators—highlighting the importance of selecting appropriate agents based on disease pathology, mechanism of action, and patient‑specific considerations in modern medical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 94
retrieved_docs.content: analogues, cardiac glycosides, vasodilators, anti‑arrhythmics and related biochemical substrates are organized under common parent classes, highlighting therapeutic overlaps, formulation types (e.g., cardioplegic solutions, ophthalmic injections) and critical safety considerations such as β‑blocker contraindications in bronchospastic disease, thereby supporting informed clinical decision‑making and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorambucil, mechlorethamine, vincristine sulfate, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlormethin hydrochloride, liposomal doxorubicin hydrochloride, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide, and pembrolizumab are all agents that may be used to treat مرض هودجكين (Hodgkin disease). These drugs represent a range of therapeutic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, mechlorethamine, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide), a microtubule inhibitor (vincristine sulfate), a DNA‑intercalating anthracycline formulation (liposomal doxorubicin hydrochloride), a bleomycin family antitumor antibiotic (bleomycin sulfate), a targeted antibody‑drug conjugate (brentuximab vedotin), a miscellaneous cytotoxic compound (chlormethin hydrochloride), and an immune checkpoint inhibitor (pembrolizumab). Their combined inclusion reflects the multimodal approach often employed in managing Hodgkin disease, where chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy may be selected based on disease stage, patient characteristics, and prior treatment response.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: Conventional alkylating and antimetabolite chemotherapies—chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, ifosfamide, mechlorethamine, procarbazine (including its hydrochloride form), dakarbazin and lomustine—exert cytotoxic effects on rapidly dividing Hodgkin lymphoma cells. Platinum‑based therapy with cisplatin and anthracycline antibiotics doxorubicin hydrochloride (both standard and liposomal formulations) add DNA‑damaging mechanisms. Additional cytotoxic agents used in Hodgkin protocols include bleomycin sulfate, vinblastine sulfate, vinorelbine tartrate and streptozocin, each targeting distinct aspects of tumor proliferation and survival. Targeted and immunomodulatory drugs expand therapeutic options: brentuximab vedotin delivers an anti‑CD30 antibody‑drug conjugate directly to Hodgkin lymphoma cells; pembrolizumab, a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor, enhances antitumor immune responses; and sargramostim, a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor, can augment host

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
retrieved_docs.content: coal‑tar 50 mg/mL medicated shampoo contains ethanol, underscoring alcohol’s role as a solvent in dermatologic formulations. The procedural hierarchy “Procedures involving the use of pharmaceuticals” includes the specific intervention “administration of inhaled nitric oxide.” Cytotoxic agents such as doxorubicin, procarbazine, ifosfamide, streptozocin, vinblastine and vinorelbine exert antitumor effects by inducing DNA damage or disrupting microtubule dynamics, while pembrolizumab provides PD‑1 checkpoint inhibition and sargramostim offers colony‑stimulating‑factor‑mediated immunomodulation. Collectively, these diverse drug classes can be used alone or in combination to personalize therapeutic strategies for patients with Hodgkin disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1401
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorambucil, lomustine (lomustin), mechlorethamine, procarbazine, bleomycin sulfate, chlormethine hydrochloride, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, hydrochloride procarbazine, pembrolizumab, vinblastine sulfate, and vinorelbine tartrate are all agents that may be used to treat Hodgkin disease (مرض هودجكين). These drugs encompass a range of therapeutic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, procarbazine, chlormethine hydrochloride), antitumor antibiotics (bleomycin sulfate), nitrogen mustard derivatives (mechlorethamine), platinum‑free cytotoxic agents (lomustine), vinca alkaloids (vinblastine sulfate, vinorelbine tartrate), and immune checkpoint inhibition (pembrolizumab). Their inclusion in treatment protocols reflects the multimodal approach required for effective management of Hodgkin disease, combining cytotoxic chemotherapy with targeted immunotherapy to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1719
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” is linked to a variety of pharmacologic agents in the context of radiation effects (účlosti záření). Specifically, PARP‑inhibitory agents, antifibrinolytics, bleomycin, cardiac glycosides, chromonar, coagulation factors (faktory krevní koagulace), hypoxanthin, and insulin Lispro are all identified as allowed qualifiers for the effects of radiation. Within this group, PARP‑inhibitory agents are further classified as a type of hypoxanthin, indicating that PARP inhibition represents a subclass of the hypoxanthin compound family. This network of relationships highlights that these diverse agents may be considered permissible modifiers when evaluating or managing radiation‑related outcomes, and it clarifies the hierarchical link between PARP inhibition and hypoxanthin chemistry.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 34
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge links a wide array of pharmacologic substances to oncology protocols, especially Hodgkin disease (مرض هودجكين), and to procedures that involve the use of pharmaceuticals. The AT protocol is mapped to the anthracycline doxorubicin, and the R‑CHOP chemotherapy regimen lists doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide as direct constituents, underscoring doxorubicin’s central role in multiple oncologic regimens. Doxorubicin, including its formulations doxorubicin hydrochloride and liposomal doxorubicin, is recognized for treating Hodgkin disease and is classified as a value for a GDC property within the broader class “Pharmacologic Substance.” The same class also includes ethinylestradiol, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlorambucil, chlormethin hydrochloride, cisplatina (cisplatin), cyclophosphamide anhydrous, cyklofosfamid (cyclophosphamide), dakarbazin (procarbazine), hydrochlorid prokarbazinu (procarbazine hydrochloride), ifosfamid (ifosfamide), barbituráty,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 108
retrieved_docs.content: AT protocol is linked to the anthracycline agent doxorubicin, and the R‑CHOP chemotherapy regimen lists doxorubicin (including its hydrochloride and liposomal formulations) as a direct component; R‑CHOP also incorporates the Vinca‑alkaloid derivative vinkristin. Doxorubicin, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlorambucil, chlormethine hydrochloride, cisplatin, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, dakarbazin, ifosfamide, procarbazine (hydrochloride and liposomal forms), lomustine, mechlorethamine, streptozocin, vinblastine, vinblastine sulfate, vincristine sulfate, vinorelbine (including its tartrate), and Vintafolide are all classified as pharmacologic substances. Within this class, hormonal agents such as ethinylestradiol and hydroxyprogesterone caproate, barbiturates, and nitrosoureas are also included, illustrating the broad scope of the “Pharmacologic Substance” classification, which is associated with procedures involving the use of pharmaceuticals (e.g., administration of inhaled
